title: Boeing (BA) Intrinsic Value
description: An independent discounted cash flow (DCF) intrinsic value analysis of The Boeing Company (BA) compiled entirely by Gemini 3.1.

fcfGrowth.title: FCF Growth Rate (Y1-Y5)
fcfGrowth.rationale: A highly conservative 5.0% FCF growth rate accounts for the ongoing struggle to ramp up production of the 737 MAX amidst FAA scrutiny and supply chain woes, slightly offset by strong defense contracts and long-term commercial backlogs.

discountRate.title: Discount Rate (WACC)
discountRate.rationale: A 9.0% discount rate is utilized to heavily discount the substantial execution risk, regulatory uncertainty, and recent execution missteps, requiring a higher return to justify the elevated risk profile compared to an index.

terminalGrowth.title: Terminal Growth Rate
terminalGrowth.rationale: A 2.0% terminal growth rate assumes Boeing maintains its duopoly position long-term, growing roughly in line with baseline global economic expansion as commercial air travel demand normalizes.

thesisTitle: The Turnaround Thesis: Trapped Value in a Duopoly
thesisParagraphs: Boeing’s position as one half of a global duopoly in large commercial aircraft manufacturing should theoretically make it a cash-printing machine. The backlog of orders is immense, guaranteeing years of revenue visibility. However, the company has repeatedly stumbled over its own feet. Between the protracted 737 MAX grounding, ongoing 787 delivery pauses, and stumbles in its space and defense businesses, execution has been woeful. Management is currently prioritizing regulatory compliance and cultural reform over short-term financial engineering.

The valuation hinges almost entirely on management's ability to execute a successful turnaround. If Boeing can stabilize its supply chain, satisfy regulators, and steadily increase its monthly production rates, the resulting surge in free cash flow will be substantial. At its current price of around $201, the market is pricing in a relatively smooth recovery. Our conservative DCF, which heavily discounts near-term cash flows due to execution risk, suggests the stock is moderately overvalued until there is clear evidence that the operational bleeding has stopped and sustainable free cash flow generation has returned.
verdictFooter: Based on FY2025 actuals. Data via S&P Global (Mar 20, 2026).

faq.question: Why did Gemini pick a 5% FCF growth rate for Boeing?
faq.answer: While the order backlog is massive, actual cash flow depends on deliveries. Gemini modeled a conservative 5% growth rate because Boeing faces ongoing regulatory caps on production and persistent supply chain bottlenecks that limit its ability to rapidly accelerate deliveries.

faq.question: Why is the discount rate for Boeing set at 9.0%?
faq.answer: A higher discount rate reflects the elevated risk profile of the company. Recent quality control issues, regulatory scrutiny, and high debt levels require a larger margin of safety and a higher required rate of return for investors taking on that risk.

faq.question: Does this model factor in Boeing's defense business?
faq.answer: Yes. While the commercial segment is the primary driver of value (and volatility), the model incorporates cash flows from Boeing Defense, Space & Security, which provides a stabilizing, albeit lower-margin, revenue floor.
